county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1879 from Yavapai County. Navajo County carved from Apache in 1895. Contains portions of the Navajo Nation, Fort Apache Indian Reservation (White Mountain Apache), and Zuni Reservation.
county_metadata.research_tips: Apache County encompasses vast tribal lands — the Navajo Nation covers most of the northern portion while the White Mountain Apache Reservation dominates the south. Mormon settlers founded St. Johns, Springerville, and Eagar in the 1870s-1880s; LDS ward records are essential for these communities. Clerk of Superior Court in St. Johns handles probate and marriage records. County Recorder has deeds from 1879. Check Navajo Nation records office in Window Rock for tribal members. The Arizona State Library, Archives & Public Records (ASLAPR) in Phoenix holds microfilmed territorial-era records. Pre-1879 records are in Yavapai County, Prescott.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Index and images available through county recorder office; limited online access

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed books from 1879 to present. Continuous coverage from county formation.

source_profiles.repository: Clerk of Superior Court, St. Johns
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Marriage licenses from 1879. Arizona required licenses statewide from territorial era.

source_profiles.repository: Clerk of Superior Court, St. Johns
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Wills, estates, guardianships from 1879.

source_profiles.repository: Clerk of Superior Court, St. Johns
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Civil and criminal case files, including naturalization records from territorial era.

source_profiles.source_name: LDS Ward Records — Eastern Arizona Stakes
source_profiles.coverage_notes: St. Johns Ward, Springerville Ward, and Round Valley Stake records. Membership, blessings, baptisms, marriages.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Well preserved in LDS Church archives

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Federal census 1880-1950. 1870 census captured as part of Yavapai County. Navajo Nation members enumerated on separate Indian schedules.
Cemetery transcriptions from St. Johns, Springerville, Eagar, and rural cemeteries. Coverage of reservation cemeteries is limited.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1881 from Pima County. Named for Chiricahua Apache chief Cochise. Tombstone was original county seat until 1929 when moved to Bisbee. Santa Cruz County carved from Cochise in 1899.
county_metadata.research_tips: Cochise County is synonymous with Arizona mining and frontier history — Tombstone, Bisbee, and Douglas were major mining towns with rich newspaper and court records. The Tombstone Epitaph newspaper (from 1880) is invaluable for local history. County Recorder in Bisbee holds deeds from 1881; mining claim records are extensive. Clerk of Superior Court handles probate/marriage. The Arizona Historical Society branch in Tucson holds Cochise County manuscript collections. Fort Huachuca military records (1877-present) document Army families. Check Mexican consular records in Douglas for cross-border families. Pre-1881 records are in Pima County, Tucson.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Index and some images via county recorder; mining claims indexed separately

source_profiles.source_name: Cochise County Deed and Mining Claim Records
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed books and mining claim records from 1881. Extensive mining claim filings from Tombstone, Bisbee, and surrounding districts.
Marriage licenses from 1881. Many early marriages in mining camps.
Wills, estates, guardianships. Mining-era probate records document property and claim transfers.

source_profiles.source_name: The Tombstone Epitaph and Bisbee Daily Review
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Tombstone Epitaph from 1880 — one of Arizona's most famous newspapers. Bisbee Daily Review covers mining community. Legal notices, obituaries, mining news.
Post returns, personnel records, and Buffalo Soldier records from Fort Huachuca. Important for African American military genealogy — home of the 10th Cavalry and 92nd Infantry Division.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Holdings at NARA and fort museum

source_profiles.repository: ASLAPR, Phoenix / Clerk of Superior Court, Bisbee
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Territorial-era court records including criminal cases from Tombstone's frontier period, naturalization proceedings, and civil disputes.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Partially at ASLAPR, partially in county

source_profiles.repository: Find A Grave / Cochise County Genealogical Society
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Boothill Graveyard (Tombstone), Evergreen Cemetery (Bisbee), and numerous small mining camp cemeteries. Many unmarked graves in remote areas.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1891 from Yavapai County. Second-largest county by area in the US (18,661 sq mi). Contains Grand Canyon National Park, portions of Navajo Nation, Hopi Reservation, Havasupai Reservation, and Kaibab Paiute Reservation.
county_metadata.research_tips: Coconino is geographically enormous — second largest county in the US. Flagstaff was a railroad town (Atlantic & Pacific Railroad, later ATSF) and lumber center; railroad employment records and lumber company records are valuable sources. Northern Arizona University in Flagstaff houses Cline Library Special Collections with extensive regional manuscripts. County Recorder in Flagstaff has deeds from 1891. Multiple tribal nations within county boundaries — Navajo, Hopi, Havasupai, and Kaibab Paiute — each maintain separate records. The Arizona Historical Society Flagstaff branch has local collections. Pre-1891 records are in Yavapai County, Prescott. Grand Canyon area homesteading records at BLM General Land Office.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Index available online; images require in-person visit or request

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed books from 1891 to present. Includes ranching, lumber company, and railroad land transactions.
Marriage licenses from 1891.
Wills, estates, guardianships from 1891.

source_profiles.source_name: BLM General Land Office Records — Coconino County
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Federal land patents including homestead entries, desert land entries, and timber culture claims. Covers public domain lands not part of reservations.

source_profiles.source_name: Coconino Sun and Arizona Daily Sun
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Flagstaff newspapers covering lumber industry, railroad, ranching, and local community events. Legal notices, obituaries, and land transactions.
Federal census from 1900 (first census after county creation). 1890 census destroyed by fire. Pre-1891 enumerated under Yavapai County.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1881 from Maricopa and Pinal counties. Contains portions of Fort Apache Indian Reservation (White Mountain Apache), San Carlos Apache Reservation, and Tonto National Forest.
county_metadata.research_tips: Gila County was a major copper and silver mining center — Globe-Miami mining district records are extensive. County Recorder in Globe holds deeds from 1881; mining claim records are a primary source. The Old Dominion Mine and Inspiration Consolidated Copper Company records document many immigrant miners (Cornish, Mexican, Italian, Slavic). Clerk of Superior Court handles probate and marriage. Roosevelt Dam (built 1903-1911) construction records at NARA document workers. San Carlos Apache and White Mountain Apache reservation records are separate — contact tribal offices. The Gila County Historical Society in Globe holds local collections. Payson area in northern Gila County has separate community history — Pleasant Valley War (Tewksbury-Graham feud) records are at ASLAPR.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Limited online access; in-person research recommended

source_profiles.source_name: Gila County Deed and Mining Records
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed books and mining claim records from 1881. Globe-Miami mining district claims are extensive.
Marriage licenses from 1881.
Wills, estates, guardianships from 1881. Mining-era estates often list claim holdings.

source_profiles.repository: ASLAPR, Phoenix / Clerk of Superior Court, Globe
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Declarations of intention and naturalization petitions. Many immigrant miners from Cornwall, Mexico, Italy, and Eastern Europe naturalized through Gila County courts.
Globe's primary newspaper covering mining operations, legal notices, community events, and Apache conflict reporting.
1890 census destroyed. First surviving Gila County census is 1900. 1880 census under Maricopa/Pinal counties.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1881 from Apache and Pima counties. Greenlee County carved from Graham in 1909. Contains portions of San Carlos Apache Reservation and Fort Thomas military reservation site.
county_metadata.research_tips: Graham County has strong Mormon settlement history — Safford, Thatcher, and Pima were founded by LDS colonists in the late 1870s-1880s. LDS ward and stake records (Eastern Arizona Stake, organized 1883) are critical sources. The Graham County Historical Society in Thatcher holds local collections. County Recorder in Safford has deeds from 1881. Clerk of Superior Court handles probate and marriage records. The Gila Valley was also home to Hispanic ranching families predating Anglo settlement. Mining activity in the Aravaipa and Klondyke areas. San Carlos Apache Reservation records are administered by the tribe. Eastern Arizona College in Thatcher has local history archives. Fort Thomas military post records (1876-1891) at NARA.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Limited online access through county recorder

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed books from 1881 to present. Includes agricultural and ranching land transactions in the Gila Valley.
Marriage licenses from 1881. Many early marriages among Mormon settlers.
Wills, estates, guardianships from 1881.

source_profiles.source_name: LDS Ward Records — Gila Valley / St. Joseph Stake
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Safford Ward, Thatcher Ward, Pima Ward, and other Gila Valley LDS congregations. Membership, blessings, baptisms, marriages, patriarchal blessings.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Well preserved in LDS Church archives

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Federal census from 1890 (destroyed). First surviving enumeration 1900. Pre-1881 area under Apache/Pima counties.
Post returns and personnel records from Fort Thomas military reservation on the Gila River. Records of Apache campaigns.

county_metadata.record_losses: 1906 and 1916 Clifton floods damaged some early county and community records
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1909 from Graham County. Named for Mason Greenlee, early settler. Last county created in Arizona Territory before statehood.
county_metadata.research_tips: Greenlee County is Arizona's smallest by population and dominated by copper mining — the Clifton-Morenci mining district was among the world's largest copper producers. Phelps Dodge (now Freeport-McMoRan) company records are essential for tracing mining families. The workforce was heavily Mexican, Italian, and Eastern European — naturalization records are key. Clifton suffered major floods in 1891, 1903, 1906, and 1916 that damaged community records. County Recorder in Clifton has deeds from 1909; pre-1909 records in Graham County. Clerk of Superior Court handles probate and marriage. The 1903 Clifton-Morenci-Metcalf orphan abduction incident (Irish orphans removed from Mexican Catholic families) generated significant court records. Greenlee County Historical Society holds local collections.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed books from 1909. Mining claims and company town property records.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Some flood damage to early records; mostly intact

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Marriage licenses from 1909.
Wills and estates from 1909. Many mining accident-related estate proceedings.

source_profiles.repository: ASLAPR, Phoenix / Clerk of Superior Court, Clifton
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Declarations and petitions from immigrant copper miners — Mexican, Italian, Slavic, and Cornish origins.
Clifton's newspaper covering mining operations, labor disputes (1915 strike), accidents, and community events in the copper camps.
First enumeration as Greenlee County in 1910. Pre-1909 records under Graham County. Ethnically diverse mining workforce well documented.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1983 from Yuma County — Arizona's newest county. Named for the historic mining town of La Paz. Contains Colorado River Indian Reservation (CRIT — Mohave, Chemehuevi, Hopi, Navajo) and portions of Yuma Proving Ground.
county_metadata.research_tips: La Paz County is Arizona's newest (1983), so pre-1983 records are in Yuma County, Yuma. The Colorado River Indian Tribes (CRIT) Reservation is a major presence — contact the CRIT Museum and Library in Parker for tribal records. County Recorder in Parker has deeds from 1983; Yuma County Recorder has earlier records. Quartzsite, a major winter visitor destination, complicates modern record research. The historic town of La Paz (1862 gold rush) was once one of Arizona's largest settlements but became a ghost town — early territorial mining records at ASLAPR. Ehrenberg was an important Colorado River steamboat landing. Parker Dam and irrigation records at the Bureau of Reclamation. The Bill Williams River area has early ranching records.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Records from 1983 at county recorder; earlier records in Yuma County

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed books from 1983 (county formation). Pre-1983 deeds in Yuma County.
Marriage licenses from 1983. Pre-1983 marriages filed in Yuma County.
Wills and estates from 1983. Earlier probate in Yuma County.
Tribal enrollment records, allotment records, and community documents for Mohave, Chemehuevi, Hopi, and Navajo tribal members relocated to the reservation.

source_profiles.source_name: BLM General Land Office Records — La Paz County area
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Federal land patents including homestead and desert land entries along the Colorado River and in the Harquahala mining district.

source_profiles.source_name: La Paz County Area Federal Census Records
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Enumerated under Yuma County through 1980 census. Search Yuma County for all federal census records. CRIT reservation enumerated separately.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1871 from Yavapai County. Contains Phoenix, state capital since 1889 (moved from Prescott). Salt River Pima-Maricopa Indian Community, Fort McDowell Yavapai Nation, and Gila River Indian Community (partially) within county. Arizona's most populous county.
county_metadata.research_tips: Maricopa County is by far Arizona's most populous, containing Phoenix and the surrounding metro area. The County Recorder in Phoenix has extensive deed records from 1871 — searchable online through the county's recording system. Clerk of Superior Court handles probate and marriage, with a large volume of records. ASLAPR (Arizona State Library, Archives & Public Records) is located in Phoenix and is the primary state repository — essential for any Arizona research. Arizona Historical Society Tempe branch (formerly Papago Park) covers central Arizona. Salt River Pima-Maricopa Indian Community and Gila River Indian Community maintain tribal records. Valley canals and irrigation district records document agricultural settlement. Phoenix city directories from 1892 at ASLAPR. Territorial-era records (1871-1912) are extensive. Pre-1871 records in Yavapai County.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Comprehensive online index and images from 1871 to present through county recorder portal

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed books from 1871 to present. Online access to grantor/grantee index and document images. Largest volume of deed records in Arizona.
Marriage licenses from 1871. Largest county marriage record collection in Arizona.
Wills, estates, guardianships from 1871. Extensive probate holdings for the state's largest county.
Annual city directories listing residents, occupations, and addresses. Critical for tracking urban families between census years.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Microfilmed at ASLAPR; digitized on Ancestry

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Phoenix's primary newspaper. Legal notices, obituaries, social columns, and community events. Arizona Gazette also available.
Territorial-era court records including naturalization proceedings, criminal cases, and civil disputes. Phoenix became territorial capital in 1889.

source_profiles.repository: Find A Grave / Arizona State Genealogical Society
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Pioneer and Military Memorial Park (Phoenix), Greenwood Cemetery, Mesa Cemetery, and numerous others across the metro area.
